topic: Two people discover they've been working on identical projects
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elias stared at the blueprints spread across the worn oak table, the coffee growing cold beside his elbow. The schematic for the chronometer assembly—the intricate gearing, the precise tolerances—was undeniably the same as the one he’d finalized three weeks ago. A cold knot tightened in his chest, spreading outwards. He traced a line on the diagram with a trembling finger, his breath catching in his throat. Who else had access to this level of detail? He pushed back from the table, the scrape of his chair loud in the quiet workshop. His hands began to shake slightly as he looked around the room, suddenly hyper-aware of every tool, every misplaced wrench. He needed to verify something, anything, but the realization hung heavy in the air, suffocating the usual focus he possessed.

[story 2]

"This is impossible," Maya whispered, her voice thin and reedy. She ran a hand through her hair, the gesture jerky and uncontrolled. The code repository on her screen displayed the same sequence of algorithms, the same commented-out sections, the identical debugging logs. A sudden wave of disorientation washed over her, making the fluorescent lights above seem to buzz with an aggressive frequency. She leaned back in her chair, gripping the edge until her knuckles turned white. Every muscle in her body felt tight, coiled and ready to spring, yet utterly paralyzed. She swallowed hard, tasting metallic fear on her tongue, and forced herself to look back at the terminal, willing the screen to display something different, anything to dispel the uncanny familiarity of the work.

[story 3]

The silence in the studio was oppressive, thick enough to chew. David slowly lifted his head from the workbench, his gaze fixed on the prototype casing resting beneath a cloth. It was flawless, identical to the mock-ups he’d spent the last fortnight perfecting. A profound sense of wrongness settled over him, a deep, internal tremor that radiated through his limbs. He took a deliberate step backward, bumping against a stack of obsolete manuals. His jaw tightened, a hard line forming where softness used to reside. He cleared his throat, the sound scratchy and uneven. "No," he managed, the word barely audible. He needed air, needed distance, needing to shake off the unsettling symmetry of his own creation.
